title: Stock Market and No-Dividend Stocks
abstract: We develop a stationary model of the aggregate stock market featuring both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks within a familiar, parsimonious consumption-based equilibrium framework. We find that such a simple feature leads to profound implications supporting several stock market empirical regularities that leading consumption-based asset pricing models have difficulty reconciling. Namely, the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market leads to a lower correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate, a nonmonotonic and even negative relation between the stock market risk premium and its volatility, and a downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia.

article.introduction:
The aggregate stock market containsboth dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks.1Existing asset pricing models, however, overlook this composition and typically model the stock market as being made up of only dividend-paying stocks. We provide the first general equilibrium model of the stock market in which both types of stocks coexist. We show that this simple feature leads to profound implications that support several empirical regularities of the stock market that leading consumption-based asset pricing models such as the habit-formation model (Campbell and Cochrane (1999)), the long-run risk model (Bansal and Yaron (2004)), and the rare disaster models (Rietz (1988), Barro (2006)) have difficulty reconciling. In particular, our model can explain, (i) the low correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate (Cochrane and Hansen (1992), Campbell and Cochrane (1999), Albuquerque et al. (2016), Heyerdahl-Larsen and Illeditsch (2017)), (ii) the mixed evidence on the relation between the stock market conditional risk premium and volatility (Campbell (1987), Glosten, Jagannathan, and Runkle (1993), Whitelaw (2000), Harvey (2001), and Brandt and Kang (2004) find a negative relation, while French, Schwert, and Stambaugh (1987), Scruggs (1998), Ghysels, Santa-Clara, and Valkanov (2005), Bali and Peng (2006), Guo and Whitelaw (2006), and Ludvigson and Ng (2007) find a positive relation), and (iii) the downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia (van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012), van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017)).
Why does stock market composition matter? It should not matter much if both types of stocks have similar risk-return profiles, because in this case, the behavior of the aggregate stock market would be similar to that of dividend-paying stocks as in standard models. However, empirical evidence (highlighted below) reveals considerable differences between the risk-return behavior of dividend-paying and no-dividend stock returns: stocks that pay no dividends have lower risk premia but higher return volatilities and market betas than comparable stocks that pay dividends. In this paper, we argue that the presence of dividend-paying stocks along with their risk-return differences from those of no-dividend stocks could help explain the somewhat puzzling empirical regularities of the stock market discussed above. Toward that end, we develop a model of the aggregate stock market that features both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks within a familiar consumption-based general equilibrium framework. In addition to supporting the empirical evidence above on the aggregate stock market, the model supports evidence on the cross-sectional differences between typical dividend-paying and no-dividend stock returns. We also provide simple intuition for the underlying economic mechanisms at play and closed-form solutions for all quantities of interest. That being said, our simple framework does not generate a sufficiently rich stochastic discount factor, which limits its ability to quantitatively match the stock market moments that the leading consumption-based asset pricing models above can match.
Our stock market model is motivated by the observation that, in contrast to dividend-paying stocks, no-dividend stocks have several features that investors would need to take into account when determining prices. First, the absence of current dividends introduces information incompleteness, and thus the eventual future dividends of no-dividend stocks must be estimated using other fundamental information. Second, the absence of dividends increases uncertainty about future dividend payment periods. Third, no-dividend stocks do not contribute directly to aggregate consumption and hence to the stochastic discount factor. Accordingly, we adopt a standard, workhorse, infinite-horizon, dynamic pure-exchange economy in which the stock market contains both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks at all times, as in reality. We achieve a stationary setting by considering standard dividend stocks that pay dividends at all times and two other types of stocks that pay dividends in alternating random periods that are governed by a Poisson process. In the absence of dividends, we employ standard Bayesian filtering theory to estimate the future dividend distribution of no-dividend stocks using other fundamental information and obtain an estimated pseudo-dividend process. This filtering process induces additional variation in the estimated pseudo-dividend by making it more volatile than the underlying process that would have been used under complete information. To highlight these features and our intuition, our multistock model is deliberately simple and parsimonious in the sense that the economy comprises a single investor with standard constant relative risk aversion (CRRA) preferences and an aggregate consumption growth rate with constant mean and volatility.
Our model generates rich equilibrium implications. We first show that while the price of dividend-paying stocks is driven by their dividends, the price of no-dividend stocks is driven by their estimated pseudo-dividends in the absence of dividends. Moreover, the presence of no-dividend stocks generates a novel spillover effect in that the expected future dividend payment times of no-dividend stocks affect the prices of all stocks, including the stocks that pay dividends at all times. This result follows from the fact that the expected dividend payment times are also when the stochastic discount factor shocks are anticipated to change, and what part of the future dividend is expected to be discounted under which stochastic discount factor matters for all stock prices.
We next demonstrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market leads to a lower correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate. This result arises because the stock market consists of stocks that do not currently pay dividends and hence do not contribute to current aggregate consumption but do contribute to fluctuations in stock market returns. When we quantify this effect in our model, we see that its magnitude can be economically large. In particular, if we set the no-dividend relative stock size in our model to the long-run average of the no-dividend relative stock size in the U.S. data over the 1927 to 2011 period (Hartzmark and Solomon (2013), 21.3%), this correlation is 0.53. When we set it to the average relative size of the no-payout stocks (i.e., no dividends or share repurchases) during the 1984 to 2003 period in the United States (Boudoukh et al. (2007), 14.2%), this correlation is 0.75. In contrast, this correlation is typically close to one in leading asset pricing models. The results therefore illustrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks can help explain a meaningful part of the relatively low correlation documented by prior empirical studies, as highlighted above.
To study the relation between the stock market's conditional risk premium and volatility, we first look at the price dynamics of the individual stocks that make up the stock market. We show that the risk premium of a no-dividend stock is lower than that of an otherwise identical dividend-paying stock. This is because in the absence of dividends, the price of no-dividend stocks is driven by their estimated pseudo-dividend, which does not contribute directly to aggregate consumption and hence comoves less with aggregate consumption compared to dividend-paying stocks. The investor therefore requires a lower risk premium to hold no-dividend stocks in equilibrium, since in our model, as in standard consumption-based models, a stock's risk premium is proportional to the covariance of its returns with the aggregate consumption growth rate. This result is consistent with abundant cross-sectional empirical evidence documenting that stocks that pay no dividends (or no payouts including share repurchases) have lower average returns than comparable dividend-paying stocks (Christie (1990), Naranjo, Nimalendran, and Ryngaert (1998), Fuller and Goldstein (2011), Hartzmark and Solomon (2013)).
We also demonstrate that a no-dividend stock commanding a lower risk premium does not necessarily imply that its returns are less volatile or that it has a lower market beta than a comparable dividend stock. On the contrary, we show that no-dividend stocks have a more volatile return and a higher market beta than comparable dividend-paying stocks. This result obtains because the price of no-dividend stocks is driven by their estimated pseudo-dividend, and the estimation process, which is necessary given the absence of dividends, induces additional variation. This additional variation in no-dividend stock returns also makes their returns contribute more to, and comove more with, the aggregate stock market than comparable dividend-paying stocks, leading to a higher market beta for no-dividend stocks. These results are also consistent with cross-sectional empirical evidence documenting that stocks that pay no dividends have higher return volatility (Naranjo, Nimalendran, and Ryngaert (1998), Pástor and Veronesi (2003), Hartzmark and Solomon (2013)) and a higher market beta (Boudoukh et al. (2007), Fuller and Goldstein (2011)) than comparable dividend-paying stocks. We also offer an alternative interpretation of the no-dividend (dividend) stocks in our model as growth (value) stocks, because a typical growth stock is one with a low fundamental-to-price ratio while also sharing the three key features of no-dividend stocks in our model. Under this interpretation, our findings are consistent with the documented empirical regularities for growth and value stocks, as growth stocks have lower mean returns, higher return volatilities, and higher market betas than value stocks (e.g., Lettau and Wachter (2007)).
We further show that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market leads to a nonmonotonic and even negative relation between the conditional risk premium and volatility of the stock market. This is because the stock market risk premium is a weighted average of the risk premia of the stocks that make up the stock market. With no-dividend stocks, which command a low risk premium but high volatility, present in the stock market, the stock market risk premium is nonmonotonically related to and in particular decreasing in its volatility for sufficiently high relative size of no-dividend stocks. This result sheds light on the decidedly mixed empirical findings on this relation, also referred to as “the risk-return trade-off” (see above). Indeed, our result is in line with the empirical findings of Rossi and Timmermann (2010), who find a nonmonotonic relation between the conditional risk premium and volatility (a positive relation for low and medium levels of volatility and a negative relation for high levels of volatility) and argue that the lack of consensus in the earlier empirical literature may be due to this nonmonotonic relation.
Finally, we demonstrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market can lead to a downward-sloping term structure of the stock market risk premium by showing that short-term assets—claims to short-term aggregate dividends—tend to command a higher mean return than the stock market. This is because a short-term asset is more like a dividend-paying stock since no-dividend stocks begin paying out dividends only after some time (which may even be after the short-term asset matures). Since the mean return of a dividend stock is higher than that of a comparable no-dividend stock, this leads to a higher mean return for the short-term asset as compared to the stock market, consistent with the empirical evidence discussed above.
In summary, our model generates the following novel testable predictions for aggregate stock market returns: The higher the relative market capitalization of no-dividend stocks in the stock market, (i) the lower the correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate, (ii) the more likely the relation between the conditional risk premium and the volatility of the stock market return is negative, and (iii) the more likely the term structure of equity risk premia is downward sloping (i.e., the short-term asset has a higher mean return than the stock market). When we quantify these effects in our model, we find that they are economically significant.
Our work is related to the literature on the correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate. As discussed earlier, this correlation appears to be weak in the data and leading consumption-based asset pricing models have difficulty in reconciling this evidence. Hence, this finding is sometimes referred to as the “low correlation puzzle,” and is pointed out by behavioral theories as one of the main shortcomings of the consumption-based asset pricing framework (e.g., Barberis, Huang, and Santos (2001)). Recently, Albuquerque et al. (2016) and Heyerdahl-Larsen and Illeditsch (2017) develop consumption-based models with a single stock and demand shocks that arise from time-variation in investors' rate of time preference to reconcile this finding. Our result here complements these studies by offering a simple, yet novel, alternative explanation for this low correlation. The low correlation may also arise in models in which aggregate consumption is partially funded by labor income, as in Santos and Veronesi (2006). Our result demonstrates that in addition to labor income, the relative size of no-dividend stocks also matters for this correlation.
Our paper is also related to the vast literature on the relation between the conditional risk premium and the volatility of the stock market. As discussed above, numerous studies empirically study this relation, but evidence on the sign of the relation is mixed. On the theory side, a number of studies, using a single-stock setup, demonstrate that a nonmonotonic relation can arise in equilibrium if there is time-variation in state variables or investment opportunities (Abel (1988), Backus and Gregory (1993), Veronesi (2000), Whitelaw (2000)). Our contribution is to illustrate that a nonmonotonic relation can arise in equilibrium for an alternative reason: The stock market also consists of no-dividend stocks that have relatively low mean returns but high return volatility.
Our analysis is also related to the growing literature on the shape of the term structure of equity risk premia. As discussed earlier, this term structure appears to be downward sloping, which is somewhat puzzling since it goes against the implications of numerous leading asset pricing models. Indeed, van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012) show that the term structure of equity risk premia is upward sloping in the habit formation model of Campbell and Cochrane (1999) and in the long-run risk model of Bansal and Yaron (2004), while it is flat in the rare disaster model of Gabaix (2012). Several recent theoretical studies reconcile this finding by generating a downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia via alternative mechanisms (Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015), Croce, Lettau, and Ludvigson (2015), Hasler and Marfè (2016)). We complement this literature by demonstrating that a downward-sloping term structure can easily arise when the stock market consists of stocks that do not pay dividends.
Related papers that study no-dividend stocks from an asset pricing perspective are Pástor and Veronesi (2003) and Choi et al. (2013). Our methodology and modeling of no-dividend stocks, and hence many of our results, differ considerably from both of these works, though each paper contains a result that is similar to one of our main cross-sectional results. In particular, Pástor and Veronesi (2003) study the effects of parameter uncertainty about a firm's average profitability and focus primarily on its market-to-book ratio. They find that firms that pay no dividends have more volatile returns due to learning effects, a finding similar to ours. However, different from our setting, their framework is a partial equilibrium one in which the stochastic discount factor is specified exogenously (that is, it is not affected by whether a firm pays dividends or not). Moreover, in their setting, it is not possible to consider an aggregate stock market that consists of both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks as in our model, and thus, it is not possible to obtain our key implications on the aggregate stock market or our cross-sectional implications for stock price spillovers, stock mean returns, and market beta. Choi et al. (2013) consider a production economy in which managers choose the firm's payout policy while facing nonconvex costs in adjusting dividends and investments. They solve their model numerically and show that firms with a low probability of paying dividends in the near term command risk premia close to zero, a result similar to our finding that the no-dividend stock mean return is lower than that of a dividend-paying stock. While our framework differs from theirs in several respects, one key difference is that we show that the information incompleteness associated with an absence of dividends leads to higher return volatility and market betas for no-dividend stocks, and we provide implications of these effects for the returns of the aggregate stock market.
The corporate finance literature, in contrast, focuses primarily on the optimality of firms' dividend policies. This literature poses various theories as to why some firms do not pay dividends, with these theories related to the role of taxes, firms' life-cycle concerns, catering to investor demand, and asymmetric information (e.g., signaling and agency problems) (see Allen and Michaely (2002)). Given these potential explanations are mutually exclusive, in our model, we do not specify why stocks pay or do not pay dividends, to avoid committing to one particular reason over others. In that sense, our model does not determine endogenously why firms pay or do not pay dividends. However, the advantage of our model is that it generates rich general equilibrium implications arising from the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIpresents our model of the stock market, which features both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks. SectionIIreports our results on the correlation of the stock market with aggregate consumption, while SectionIIIon the stock market risk premium-volatility relation and SectionIVon the term structure of equity risk premia. SectionVdiscusses the model parameter values employed in our figures and quantitative statements. SectionVIconcludes. AppendixAcontains proofs. In AppendixB, we discuss the effects of differences in firm characteristics.
article.I. Stock Market with No-Dividend Stocks:
In this section, we present our model of the aggregate stock market, which consists of both dividend-paying stocks and no-dividend stocks. Our model is motivated by the observation that, in contrast to dividend-paying stocks, no-dividend stocks have several noteworthy features that investors would need to take into account when determining prices. First, no-dividend stocks' absence of current dividends introduces information incompleteness, and hence their future dividends must be estimated using other fundamental information. Second, the absence of dividends increases uncertainty about no-dividend stocks' future dividend payment periods. Third, no-dividend stocks do not contribute directly to aggregate consumption and hence to the stochastic discount factor. Since no-dividend stocks are part of the aggregate stock market, these features turn out to have important implications for stock market returns, as we demonstrate below.
The economy is cast in a stationary setting in the sense that there are dividend-paying stocks and no-dividend stocks at all times, as in reality, and our implications are not transitory and hold in all periods of the economy. The stationary structure admits much tractability in the analysis and is achieved by considering a stock market that contains the standard type of stocks that pay dividends throughout and also two other types of stocks that pay dividends in an alternating manner.2
The alternating periods are governed by the arrival times0<τ1<τ2<…of the independent Poisson processNtwith intensityλ. Without loss of generality, we assume that stock 2 pays dividends during the random periods[τn,τn+1), wherenis an odd number and denote its set of dividend-paying odd-numbered periods byTo≡[τ1,τ2)∪[τ3,τ4)∪…. Similarly, stock 3 pays dividends during[τn,τn+1), wherenis an even number and its set of dividend-paying even-numbered periods is denoted byTe≡[0,τ1)∪[τ2,τ3)∪…. We refer to stockithat is not currently paying dividends, wherei=2inTeandi=3inTo, as theno-dividend stockand the unobservable processesDiwith dynamics (2) or (3) during these periods aspseudo-dividends. We note that sinceTo∪Te=TandTo∩Te=∅, at any given timet∈T, there is one no-dividend stock and two dividend-paying stocks in our model, leading to a stationary dividend payment structure. Moreover, in our model as in reality, the no-dividend stock only accumulates capital gains/losses, as opposed to a dividend stock that also delivers a dividend yield.4The individual stock pricesSi,i=1,2,3, and hence the stock market levelSt=∑i=13Sit, are determined endogenously in equilibrium. A riskless bond in zero net supply is also available for trading.
Remark 1. (Nonrandom dividend periods)In our specification, the investor does not know when exactly the current no-dividend stock will start paying dividends or the duration of each dividend period since the alternating periods are determined by the arrival times of a Poisson process. In particular, during any period[τn,τn+1), the investor views the next arrival timeτn+1as an independent random variable with an exponential distribution. Moreover, due to the well-known memoryless property of the exponential distribution, the dividend-alternating time becomes time-independent, which then leads to stationary, constant discount terms for the stock prices as we discuss in SectionII. That being said, all of our main results continue to hold in an alternative specification in which the dividend-alternating timesτnare nonrandom known constants. In this case, however, we lose the stationary property as the discount terms for the stock prices would depend on time.

The posterior varianceVitof the no-dividend stock (stock 2 duringTe, stock 3 duringTo) is deterministic and converges to its constant nonzero steady-state value, denoted byVi∞, in the long run (see (A8) in AppendixA). To ensure that learning is optimal in the sense that it leads to more precise estimates over time, we set the exogenous prior variance such that it is greater than the steady-state posterior variance, that is,Viτn>Vi∞. One notable implication of Lemma1is that the estimation necessitated by the absence of dividends induces additional variation in the estimated pseudo-dividendD̂idynamics (6), making it more volatile than the underlying pseudo-dividendDi(see also (A10) and (A11) in AppendixA). This is because there is additional uncertainty about the mean estimate of the pseudo-dividendD̂icaptured by the posterior varianceVitthat would not be present had the dividends been observable. This additional uncertainty amplifies shocks to the fundamental news process during the estimation and leads to a more volatile estimate of the pseudo-dividend.
That the steady-state posterior variance is less than the prior variance and that the estimation leads to a more volatile process not only make sense economically, but are also present in models of learning about a constant parameter, say, the expected dividend growth rateμi. In these models, the posterior variance declines over time and converges to zero in the steady state since the investor eventually learns the true parameter value (e.g., Brennan (1998), Pástor and Veronesi (2003), Cvitanić et al. (2006), Collin-Dufresne, Johannes, and Lochstoer (2016)). However, different from these models of parameter uncertainty, in our setting, the investor learns about a nonstationary stochastic process,Di, and stops learning at a (random) timeτn+1once the dividends become observable, since this leads to complete information.9Nevertheless, our model implications would go through under parameter uncertainty if we were to instead assume that investors can observe the dividend levelsDibut not their expected growth ratesμi. However, we believe that it would be hard to justify the assumption of observable dividends for no-dividend stocks, which is our main focus. Moreover, since the posterior mean, that is, the estimated expected growth rate, is typically time-varying in the case of parameter uncertainty, we would lose the simple (Gordon growth model) stock price structures of Proposition1in SectionII.

Their difference can be thought of as the investor's implicit nonfinancial income (such as labor and government transfers). This specification is both consistent with the data, since the aggregate dividend is only a fraction of aggregate consumption (Santos and Veronesi (2006)), and present in numerous asset pricing models, including Campbell and Cochrane (1999), Brennan and Xia (2001), Bansal and Yaron (2004), and Barberis et al. (2015). We note that sometimes we make comparisons with a comparable benchmark economy in which all three stocks pay dividends with dynamics (1) to (3). Remark 3. (Stock dividends, output, and aggregate consumption)Our specification of the aggregate consumption dynamics is in the spirit of Lucas (1978), who models stocks as claims to the trees whose output (dividends) are perishable and must be consumed in the given period. Under this setup, if a stock currently does not pay dividends, it does not contribute to current aggregate consumption. We capture this economic mechanism—which is key to our analysis—in a tractable way through the constant sensitivities, which, in turn, lead to constant mean and volatility of the aggregate consumption growth rate in our economy, as (11) illustrates. This formulation simplifies the analysis by leading stock prices to be as in the Gordon growth model in the comparable benchmark economy, as we discuss in SectionIIbelow.
In our model, the aggregate consumption growth rate (10) is i.i.d. since it loads on two i.i.d. dividend growth rates with constant weightsαk. This is in contrast to the somewhat similar “two-trees” model of Cochrane, Longstaff, and Santa-Clara (2007), in which the aggregate consumption growth rate is non-i.i.d. since it loads on two i.i.d. dividend growth rates with time-varying weights equal to the relative shares of two dividends. The latter specification can generate additional aggregate consumption dynamics but it also leads to an analytically intractable equilibrium and possibly a nonstationary one in the long run since one tree can dominate in the limit. In contrast, by assuming constant weights (sensitivities), our setting does not account for the effects of time-variation in the relative shares of dividends in aggregate consumption, but it ensures much tractability and leads to a stationary consumption process over time. Moreover, one may be tempted to conclude that the no-dividend stock in our model can also be thought of as the limiting case of a low-dividend-paying stock in the two-tree model. This would not be correct, however, since in the two-tree model, no matter how small the dividend is, it still provides a valuable signal and there is no information incompleteness.
article.II. Stock Market Correlation with Consum:
In this section, we investigate how the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market affects the correlation of the stock market with aggregate consumption in equilibrium. We first demonstrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks generates a novel spillover effect in that the expected dividend payment period affects the prices of all stocks. We then show that the presence of such stocks leads to a lower correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate, consistent with the well-known empirical regularity.
Equilibrium in our economy is defined in a standard way. The economy is said to be inequilibriumif the equilibrium consumption, portfolio strategy, and stock and bond prices are such that the investor chooses her optimal consumption and portfolio strategy and the good, stock, and bond markets clear. One difficulty in obtaining equilibrium stock prices in our model is that shocks to the stochastic discount factor alternate countably infinite times at the random arrival times of the Poisson processNt. We solve for the equilibrium stock prices by considering the countably finite case and then taking the limit. In doing so, we make use of the stationary increments property of Poisson processes, which leads to a recursive relationship among each random period's contribution to the stock price. The tractability of our model leads to closed-form solutions for all economic quantities, as presented in our propositions.
Proposition1presents the equilibrium stock prices.11In what follows, the mean stock returnsri,i=1,2,3, are defined byEt[(dSit+Ditdt)/Sitdt]for dividend stocks (i.e.,i=1,3inTe,i=1,2inTo) and byEt[dSit/Sitdt]for no-dividend stocks (i.e.,i=2inTe,i=3inTo).
Proposition 1. (Equilibrium stock prices)The equilibrium stock market level and individual stock pricesi=1,2,3in the benchmark economy with all dividend stocks are given by


In the benchmark economy in which all stocks in the stock market pay dividends, each individual equilibrium stock price is driven by its current dividendsDit, as in standard asset pricing models. In our setup, these prices follow the simple Gordon growth model with the constant discount terms given by the mean stock returns net of dividend growth rates. In our economy in which no-dividend stocks are also present, the individual equilibrium stock prices still have simple structures but they differ in two respects. First, while the prices of dividend-paying stocks continue to be driven by their current dividendsDit, the prices of no-dividend stocks are now driven by their estimated pseudo-dividendD̂itin the absence of dividends. Second, all individual stock prices now have additional terms that adjust for changes in the equilibrium stochastic discount factor shocks at the random dividend-alternating times governed by the arrival timesτnof the Poisson processNtwith intensityλ. Naturally, these differences in individual stock prices are reflected in the stock market level as it is the sum of the individual stock prices.
The additional terms in our economy reveal that the no-dividend stock's expected dividend payment times that are determined byλaffect not only its own price but also the prices of all other stocks including the first stock that pays dividends at all times. This is because the expected dividend payment times correspond to when the shocks to aggregate consumption and stochastic discount factor are anticipated to change. Since stock prices are the total expected discounted future dividends, the part of future dividends that is expected to be discounted under the odd-numbered periodToversus the even-numbered periodTestochastic discount factors affects their prices. This spillover effect is not present in the benchmark economy in which each stock pays dividends at all times and each stock's price depends only on its own parameters, apart from the obvious indirect dependence through its endogenous equilibrium mean return (Proposition3). While the spillover of a no-dividend stock's expected dividend payment times to other stock prices due to a simple economic mechanism, to the best of our knowledge, this result is novel and has not been demonstrated previously in the literature.12

In the benchmark economy with all dividend stocks, a shock to any dividendDi,i=1,2,3leads to fluctuations in both aggregate consumption and stock market returns. In turn, this leads the covariance and hence the correlation of stock market returns with the aggregate consumption growth rate to depend on all dividend growth rate variances and covariances (the numerator of (17)). However, in our economy with no-dividend stocks, shocks to aggregate consumption arise only from shocks to the dividend stocks, whereas stock market returns are additionally driven by the no-dividend stocks' estimated pseudo-dividends. Thus, the variance term in the numerator of (18) only has the dividend growth rate variances of the dividend stocks.13
The notable implication here is that the correlation of stock market returns with the aggregate consumption growth rate in our economy with no-dividend stocks is lower than that in the benchmark economy. This result is intuitive as it says that when the stocks that do not contribute to current aggregate consumption are also part of the stock market, stock market returns are less correlated with aggregate consumption. Figure1illustrates the low correlation in our economy by plotting the equilibrium correlation of aggregate stock market returns with the aggregate consumption growth rate against the no-dividend relative stock sizeSit/St. We can see that as the no-dividend stocks become more dominant in the stock market, this correlation becomes smaller. In particular, if we set the no-dividend relative stock size in our model to the long-run average of the no-dividend relative stock size in the United States over the period 1927 to 2011 as reported by Hartzmark and Solomon (2013) (21.3%), this correlation is 0.53. When we set it to the average relative size of the no-payout stocks (no dividends and no share repurchases) in the United States over the period 1984 to 2003 as reported by Boudoukh et al. (2007) (14.2%), this correlation is 0.75. In contrast, this correlation is typically close to one in leading asset pricing models (as discussed below) and in the comparable benchmark economy without no-dividend stocks, as depicted in Figure1.
As discussed in the introduction, this correlation appears to be weak in the data (Cochrane and Hansen (1992), Campbell and Cochrane (1999), Albuquerque et al. (2016), Heyerdahl-Larsen and Illeditsch (2017)), and leading consumption-based asset pricing models have difficulty reconciling this evidence. This finding is therefore sometimes referred to as the “low-correlation puzzle.” Our contribution here is to demonstrate that a significant portion of this low correlation may be due to a simple explanation that is not typically considered in standard consumption-based asset pricing models: the stock market consists of many stocks that do not currently pay dividends and hence do not contribute to current aggregate consumption or dividends, but do contribute to fluctuations in aggregate stock market returns. Therefore, it naturally follows that stock market returns, which are driven in part by fluctuations in no-dividend stocks, correlate less with the current aggregate consumption growth rate. Moreover, as we illustrate in Figure1, this effect can be quantitatively significant.
article.III. Risk Premium-Volatility Relation:
In this section, we are primarily interested in investigating how the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market affects the relation between the stock market's conditional risk premium and volatility, that is, “the risk-return trade-off,” in equilibrium. Towards that end, we first show that consistent with prior empirical evidence, a no-dividend stock's risk premium is lower, but its return volatility and market beta are higher, than those of an otherwise identical dividend stock. More notably, we then demonstrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market can generate a nonmonotonic and even negative relation between the stock market risk premium and its volatility, again consistent with the empirical evidence.
Proposition3presents the mean stock returns and their properties in equilibrium.
Proposition 3. (Equilibrium mean stock returns)The equilibrium mean returns of the stock market and individual stocksi=1,2,3in the benchmark economy are given by

Consequently, in the economy with no-dividend stocks, the risk premium of a no-dividend stock is lower than that of an otherwise identical dividend stock.

In both the benchmark economy and our economy, the equilibrium stock market risk premia,rSt−r, are simple weighted averages of the dividend and no-dividend stock risk premia. The weights are the relative sizes of the individual stocks, and each individual stock's risk premium is proportional to the covariance of its stock return with the aggregate consumption growth rate, as in standard consumption-based asset pricing models.14In the benchmark economy in which all stocks in the stock market pay dividends, each individual stock's risk premium consists of a variance component and a covariance component. The variance component is due to the fact that each stock's dividend, which drives the stock price, contributes directly to current aggregate consumption with sensitivityα¯i, which implies the risk premiumγα¯iσi2. The covariance component is due to the fact that each stock is driven by its dividend, which (potentially) comoves with the other stocks' dividends and thereby implies the risk premiumγ∑j≠i3α¯jρσiσj. In our economy, the dividend stock's risk premium again consists of variance and covariance components. However, the no-dividend stock's risk premium only has a covariance component since its estimated pseudo-dividend, which drives its price, does not contribute directly to the aggregate consumption shock, but rather only (potentially) comoves with it.
A notable implication is that a no-dividend stock's risk premium is lower than that of an otherwise identical dividend stock. This is intuitive because as discussed earlier, the no-dividend stock price is driven by its estimated pseudo-dividend, which does not contribute directly to aggregate consumption and hence comoves less with the aggregate consumption growth rate than does a comparable dividend stock. Therefore, the investor requires a lower risk premium to hold the no-dividend stock in equilibrium.15This result is consistent with cross-sectional empirical evidence, documenting that stocks that pay no dividends (or no payouts including share repurchases) have lower average returns than comparable stocks that pay dividends (Christie (1990), Naranjo, Nimalendran, and Ryngaert (1998), Fuller and Goldstein (2011), Hartzmark and Solomon (2013)).
Proposition4reports the equilibrium stock return volatilities for the stock marketσSt=Vart[dSt/Stdt]and the individual stocksσSit=Vart[dSit/Sitdt]fori=1,2,3.
Proposition 4. (Equilibrium stock return volatilities)The equilibrium volatilities of the stock market and individual stocksi=1,2,3in the benchmark economy are given by

Consequently, in the economy with no-dividend stocks, the volatility of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of an otherwise identical dividend stock.

The equilibrium stock market volatility is driven by the relative sizes of the individual stocks, which determine the extent to which each stock's volatility contributes to fluctuations in stock market returns. In the benchmark economy in which all stocks pay dividends, the return volatility of each stockiis constant and equal to the volatility of its dividend growth rate,σi. In our economy with no-dividend stocks, in addition to the volatility of the dividend growth rateσi, the volatility of a dividend stock's return is also affected by the uncertainty about the arrival of the dividend-alternating times due to the discrete changes in prices during these times. In contrast, in the absence of dividends, the return volatility of a no-dividend stock is driven by the volatility of the stock's estimated pseudo-dividend growth rate and the uncertainty about the arrival of dividend-alternating times. Therefore, the posterior varianceVitand the parameters of the fundamental news processκi,viaffect the volatility of the no-dividend stock.
Consequently, we show that the return volatility of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of an otherwise identical dividend stock.16This is intuitive because as we discuss earlier, the price of no-dividend stocks is driven by their estimated pseudo-dividend, and the estimation process required in the absence of dividends induces additional variation, which is reflected in the stock returns. This result is also consistent with the empirical evidence, documenting that stocks that pay no dividends have higher return volatility than comparable stocks that pay dividends (Naranjo, Nimalendran, and Ryngaert (1998), Pástor and Veronesi (2003), Hartzmark and Solomon (2013)).
Having determined the aggregate stock market (conditional) risk premium and volatility in Propositions3and4, we next investigate our model implications for the relation between these two quantities. Figure2, Panel A presents our findings with a scatter plot of the stock market (conditional) risk premium and volatility, where each point represents a different no-dividend relative stock sizeSit/Stwithin an empirically relevant range of [0, 0.30]. To illustrate this relation more clearly, Panel B plots the fitted values of the linear regressions of the stock market risk premium on its return volatility in our model by dividing no-dividend relative stock sizes into a low-volatility region, [0, 0.15] and a high-volatility region, [0.15, 0.30].
As Figure2, Panel A illustrates, in the benchmark economy in which all stocks pay dividends, the relation between the stock market risk premium and volatility is monotonically positive, consistent with standard intuition. However, as Figure2shows, in our economy with no-dividend stocks, this relation becomes nonmonotonic and can even be negative. This is because, as discussed above, the stock market risk premium is the (relative-size) weighted average of the corresponding risk premia of stocks that make up the stock market. Therefore, when the no-dividend stocks—stocks that command low risk premia but high volatility—are also part of the stock market, the stock market risk premium is nonmonotonically related to its volatility. In particular, the stock market risk premium is decreasing in its volatility for high volatility levels, corresponding to high relative size of the no-dividend stocks. Our prediction is in line with the empirical evidence in Rossi and Timmermann (2010), who find a nonmonotonic relation between the conditional risk premium and volatility by showing a positive relation for low and medium levels of volatility and a negative relation for high levels of volatility. As Figure2clearly demonstrates, this result and its mechanism can be economically significant. In terms of magnitudes of the risk premium and volatility, we find that when the no-dividend relative stock size in our model is equal to its long-run average in the data (21.3%), for the same risk premium of 1.31%, the volatility of the stock market is 8.51% in the benchmark economy but 15.07% in our economy. Similarly, when the relative size of no-dividend stocks is equal to the average relative size of no-payout stocks (no dividends and no share repurchases) (14.2%), for the same risk premium of 1.57%, the volatility of the stock market is 9.76% in the benchmark economy but 12.81% in our economy.17
As we discuss in the introduction, numerous empirical studies find a negative relation between the stock market conditional risk premium and volatility (e.g., Campbell (1987), Glosten, Jagannathan, and Runkle (1993), Whitelaw (2000), Harvey (2001), Brandt and Kang (2004)), while many others, consistent with the basic intuition, find this relation to be positive (e.g., French, Schwert, and Stambaugh (1987), Scruggs (1998), Ghysels, Santa-Clara, and Valkanov (2005), Bali and Peng (2006), Guo and Whitelaw (2006), Ludvigson and Ng (2007)). On the theory side, a number of studies using a single-stock setup demonstrate that a nonmonotonic and even negative relation can arise in equilibrium if there is time-variation in state variables or investment opportunities. Our contribution is to illustrate that, using a simple multiple-stock setup, nonmonotonic and negative relations can also arise in equilibrium for a very simple reason, namely, that the stock market also consists of no-dividend stocks whose mean return-volatility relation is not in line with standard intuition (low mean return but high return volatility).
In addition to its return volatility, the market beta of an individual stock is another popular measure of stock risk. To examine whether the no-dividend stock is also riskier than the comparable dividend stock when risk is measured by the market beta, in Proposition5, we present our model implications for the equilibrium market betasβSifor each individual stocki=1,2,3, whereβSit=Covt[(dSit/Sit),(dSt/St)]/Vart[dSt/St].
Proposition 5. (Equilibrium market betas of individual stocks)The equilibrium market betas of individual stocksi=1,2,3in the benchmark economy are given by

Consequently, in the economy with no-dividend stocks, the market beta of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of an otherwise identical dividend stock.

In the benchmark economy, the equilibrium market betas are in terms of the underlying risksσiand relative stock sizesS¯it/S¯t. When two stocks are otherwise identical (i.e., same underlying risks and relative stock sizes), they have the same market beta. In our economy with no-dividend stocks, the market beta of a dividend stock is also affected by uncertainty about the arrival of the dividend-alternating times due to discrete changes in stock prices during these times. The market beta of a no-dividend stock is further affected by its posterior varianceVitalong with the parametersκi,vithrough their effects on its return volatility.
As a result, the market beta of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of a comparable dividend stock. This is because returns of no-dividend stocks are more volatile (Proposition4) and hence contribute more to, and comove more with, aggregate stock market returns than comparable dividend stocks. This result is also consistent with the empirical evidence, which documents that stocks that pay no dividends (or no payouts including share repurchases) have higher market betas than comparable stocks that pay dividends (Boudoukh et al. (2007), Fuller and Goldstein (2011)).
Remark 4. (Our model's relation to value versus growth stocks)In our model, we refer to stocks that are not currently paying dividends as no-dividend stocks since they have zero dividend yield. One could also think of no-dividend (dividend) stocks in our model asgrowth (value)stocks, since in the literature, a typical growth (value) stock has a low (high) fundamental-to-price ratio, where this ratio typically is the book-to-market, earnings yield,dividend yield, or ratio of cash flows to price (Lettau and Wachter (2007)). Moreover, the three key features of no-dividend stocks in our model also hold for growth stocks. For example, growth stocks share the feature that true future dividends need to be estimated, since their current low fundamentals are not representative of their eventual significant future dividends. Second, growth stocks share the feature of having additional uncertainty about their future main dividend payment period. Third, due to their low current fundamentals, growth stocks currently contribute little to aggregate consumption and hence to the stochastic discount factor. Under this interpretation, our cross-sectional implications are also consistent with the documented empirical regularities for growth and value stocks, since as summarized in Lettau and Wachter (2007), growth stocks have lower mean returns but higher return volatilities and market betas compared to value stocks.
article.IV. Term Structure of Equity Risk Premia:
Finally, we investigate our model's implications for the shape of the term structure of stock market equity risk premia. There has been growing interest in this term structure following the findings of van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012), who study a claim on the dividends of the S&P 500 index in the near future, that is, of a short-term asset, and find that the short-term asset commands a higher average return (and Sharpe ratio) than the underlying index. The authors conclude that the term structure of equity risk premia is downward sloping. As we discuss in the introduction, this empirical finding is considered somewhat puzzling since it runs against the implications of several leading asset pricing models. Here we demonstrate that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market can generate this downward sloping term structure of equity risk premia. Toward that end, we follow van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012) and define the short-term asset as a claim to aggregate dividends up to maturityTat a timet. We present its equilibrium mean return, denoted byrSt,T, in Proposition6.18
Proposition 6. (Equilibrium short-term asset mean return)The equilibrium mean return of the short-term asset in the benchmark economy is given by

Consequently, in the economy with no-dividend stocks, the mean return of the short-term asset is higher than that of the stock market if the deterministic termhit,Tfor a dividend stock is greater than that of a no-dividend stock.

Similar to the structure of the equilibrium stock market mean return of Proposition3, the short-term asset's equilibrium mean return is a weighted average of the mean returns of the individual stocks. For the stock market, these weights are simply the relative stock sizes, whereas for the short-term asset, the weights also include the deterministic termshit,T, which represent the fraction of stockiin the short-term asset. In the benchmark economy, these fractions have simple forms and are driven by the dividend yieldr¯i−μiand the short-term asset maturityT−t. In our economy with no-dividend stocks, as (31) illustrates, these fractions are more involved and are additionally affected by the no-dividend stock' s expected dividend payment time that is governed byλ. These more complicated forms for the fractionshit,Tarise because the short-term asset is a claim to aggregate dividends up toT−t, during which time aggregate dividend (and stochastic discount factor) shocks may remain the same or may change when the no-dividend stock starts paying dividends.
Importantly, we find that in our economy with no-dividend stocks, the mean return of the short-term asset is higher than that of the stock market if the fraction of dividend stocks in the short-term asset is greater than the corresponding fraction for no-dividend stocks. This condition is satisfied for plausible parameter values since the short-term asset is more likely a dividend stock than a no-dividend stock. This is because the value of the short-term asset depends only on aggregate dividends up to its maturity, during which time the no-dividend stock may not start paying dividends, and hence, it is represented less in the short-term asset. Moreover, a dividend stock's mean return is higher than that of an otherwise identical no-dividend stock (Proposition3), and hence by placing larger weights on the stocks with higher mean returns, the short-term asset's mean return becomes higher than that of the stock market.
This result also implies a downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia as illustrated in Figure3, which plots the equilibrium risk premium of the short-term asset and the stock market against the maturity date of the short-term assetT−tin our economy. We see that the shorter is the maturity of the short-term asset, the higher is its risk premium, which monotonically approaches the stock market risk premium as its maturity increases, consistent with empirical evidence (van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012), van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017)). For the economic magnitude of the effects, we see that when the no-dividend relative stock size in our model is equal to its long-run average in the data (21.3%, Panel A) or to the average relative size of the no-payout stocks—no dividends or no share repurchases—(14.2%, Panel B), the risk premium of the short-term asset with maturity up to two years is 18% higher (1.55% versus 1.31% in Panel A) and 12% higher (1.75% versus 1.57% in Panel B) than the risk premium of the stock market, respectively. We note that even though we do not provide it for brevity, the corresponding Sharpe ratio for the short-term asset is higher than that of the stock market in our model as in the data.
Finally, we note that our model implications correspond to the unconditional slope of the term structure of equity risk premia. Recent empirical studies look at the shape of the conditional term structure by studying the time-variation and trends in this term structure (e.g., Gormsen (2018), Gormsen and Lazarus (2020)). Due to the simplicity of our model (i.e., standard CRRA preferences and i.i.d. aggregate consumption growth rate), meaningfully studying the time-variation and trends in this term structure is beyond the scope of the current setting. We leave these interesting features for future research.
article.V. Parameter Values:
To quantify the effects in our model, particularly the key results involving the aggregate stock market, in this section, we discuss the parameter values employed in our figures. We obtain parameter values primarily by matching the aggregate consumption growth rate mean and volatility in our economy to their analogues in the U.S. data in Campbell (2017). We also use U.S. stock market data from Robert Shiller's website to estimate the fundamental news process parameters.19TableIsummarizes the parameter values used. We note that the behavior of the equilibrium quantities depicted in our figures is typical and does not vary much with alternative plausible parameter values.
We start by setting the sum of the sensitivities,∑kαk=α1+α2=α1+α3, which captures the sensitivity of the aggregate consumption growth rate to the aggregate dividend in our model to the relative share of the aggregate dividend in aggregate consumption in the data, to15%(Santos and Veronesi (2006)). We decompose the sensitivities to capture the fact that dividend-paying stocks are several times larger than no-dividend stocks. Indeed, Fama and French (2001, table 3) show that the assets of a typical dividend-paying firm is 5.3 times (1,389 versus 262) that of a no-dividend stock that formerly paid dividends in their full sample. Accordingly, we set the ratio of the total sensitivity of the dividend-paying stock to the sensitivity of the no-dividend stock (which paid dividends previously in our model)—0.15/α2=0.15/α3– to 5.3, yieldingα1=0.122andα2=α3=0.028.20We then set the common correlation coefficient to simplyρ=0, treat each dividend growth rate symmetrically, and match the mean and volatility of the aggregate consumption growth rate in our economy to the corresponding values in the data, 1.74% and 1.64%, as reported in Campbell (2017). This gives a mean and volatility of each dividend growth rate ofμi=11.6%andσi=13.1%fori=1,2,3. We note that the values forμiare somewhat large but their values do not affect our main results. To choose the intensity parameter for the Poisson processλ, we use the average propensity of a no-dividend stock to start to pay dividends next year in the data, which is 10.1% in the full sample (1927 to 1999) of Fama and French (2001, table 2). Equating this value to the corresponding propensity in our model,0.101=1−e−λ, yieldsλ=0.106. With this choice, the expected next dividend payment time(1/λ)is around 10 years in our economy.
To make our quantitative statements corresponding to our figures more relevant, we set the no-dividend relative stock sizesSit/St,i=2,3, to the average value in the data. However, choosing the right no-dividend relative stock sizes is not straightforward, as numerous empirical studies document trends in firms' payout policies. For instance, dividends were the major payout choice until mid-1980s, after which share repurchases became more prevalent (e.g., Grullon and Michaely (2002); see also the survey of Farre-Mensa, Michaely, and Schmalz (2014) for more on trends in payout policy). For this reason, we employ two sources. First, we make use of the long-sample evidence in Hartzmark and Solomon (2013), who find that over the 1927 to 2011 period, when dividends are the main payout choice, no-dividend stocks account for21.3%of the aggregate stock market capitalization in the United States. Second, we use the evidence in Boudoukh et al. (2007), who report that over the 1984 to 2003 period, when share repurchases are also a significant fraction of total payouts, no-payout stocks (i.e., no dividends or no share repurchases) have an average relative market capitalization of14.2%.21With our baseline parameter values in TableI, the stock market has a risk premium of1.31%, return volatility of15.07%, and correlation with aggregate consumption of 0.53 when the no-dividend relative stock size in our model is equal to the long-run average of the no-dividend relative stock size in the data (21.3%) reported by Hartzmark and Solomon (2013). Similarly, when the no-dividend relative stock size in our model is equal to the average relative size of the no-payout stocks (no dividends or no share repurchases) in the data (14.2%) reported by Boudoukh et al. (2007), the stock market risk premium increases to1.57%, its return volatility decreases to12.81%, and its correlation with aggregate consumption increases to 0.75 (see our Figures1and2).
article.VI. Conclusion:
In this paper, we provide an analysis of an aggregate stock market that features both dividend-paying and no-dividend stocks within a familiar consumption-based general equilibrium framework. Our analysis leads to closed-form solutions for quantities of interest and profound qualitative implications that support several empirical regularities on the aggregate stock market while providing simple intuition for the underlying economic mechanisms. Most notably, we show that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market leads to a lower correlation between stock market returns and the consumption growth rate, a nonmonotonic and even negative relation between the stock market risk premium and its volatility, and a downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia. In terms of new testable predictions for aggregate stock market returns, these findings imply that: the higher the relative market capitalization of no-dividend stocks in the stock market, (i) the lower the correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate, (ii) the more likely the relation between the conditional risk premium and the volatility of stock market returns is negative, and (iii) the more likely the term structure of equity risk premia is downward sloping.
We also show that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the stock market generates a novel spillover effect in that the expected future dividend payment times of no-dividend stocks affect the prices of all stocks. Furthermore, consistent with prior cross-sectional empirical evidence, we find that no-dividend stocks command lower mean returns while having higher return volatilities and market betas than comparable stocks that pay dividends.
The framework that we employ in this paper is parsimonious in the sense that there is a single investor with standard CRRA preferences, and the aggregate consumption growth rate has constant mean and volatility. This framework can be extended along several different dimensions to study other potentially important issues such as heterogeneous investors, more exotic preferences, and more general aggregate consumption process. For instance, considering decreasing relative risk aversion rather than CRRA preferences may yield interesting implications. This is because the investor's relative risk aversion would be more sensitive to shocks to dividend stocks than to shocks to no-dividend stocks, which may help explain the findings of Fuller and Goldstein (2011) that no-dividend stocks command even lower mean returns in declining markets. We leave these considerations to future research.

article.Appendix B: Differences in Firm Characte:
In our main analysis, we quantify the effects in our model under the assumption that dividend and no-dividend stocks have the same characteristics. In reality, several firm characteristics affect the decision of whether to pay dividends. In particular, Fama and French (2001) and Denis and Osobov (2008) show that more profitable firms, firms with less investment and growth opportunities, and larger firms are more likely to pay dividends. Since these firm characteristics are likely to affect individual stock mean returns and volatility, it is of interest to decompose our cross-sectional results to examine the extent to which they are due to our mechanisms versus differences in firm characteristics. In this appendix, we discuss the effects of different firm characteristics, in particular, differences in the mean growth rate, volatility, and size of their fundamentals, on our results.25We find that differences in these firm characteristics can capture a significant portion of the difference in individual stock risk premia but only a small portion of the differences in stock volatilities and market betas between dividend and no-dividend stocks. We also find that our key results involving the aggregate stock market become more pronounced under these different characteristics.
To quantify our results under different firm characteristics, we modify our main parameter values employed in SectionVand summarized in TableIas follows. The sensitivity parameters, which essentially capture the average size of fundamentals in our model,αi,i=1,2,3, are unchanged as they already account for the fact that a typical dividend-paying stock fundamental is (5.3 times) larger than that of a typical no-dividend stock. We next set the mean growthμiand volatilityσiparameters such that the no-dividend stock fundamentals have higher mean growth rates and volatilities than those of the dividend stock, while ensuring that the aggregate consumption growth rate mean and volatility are still as in the data at1.74%and1.64%, respectively. Toward that end, we consider the case in whichμi=1.5μ1, whileσi=1.5σ1orσi=2σ1for no-dividend stocksi=2,3.26This leads to mean growth rates ofμ1=0.106andμ2=μ3=0.159, and growth rate volatilities ofσ1=0.127andσ2=σ3=0.191in the case ofσi=1.5σ1and ofσ1=0.122andσ2=σ3=0.244in the case ofσi=2σ1. All other parameter values are as presented in TableI. We conduct our analysis for the no-dividend relative stock size in our model being equal to its average value in the data, 21.3% or 14.2%.
TableBIreports the fraction of the differences in stock risk premium, volatility, and market betas in our economy that are due to our mechanisms as opposed to differences in the fundamental mean growth rates and volatilities that are also present in the benchmark economy. The table reveals that differences in firm characteristics have varying ability to help explain our cross-sectional results. A significant portion of our result that the risk premium of a no-dividend stock is lower than that of a dividend stock,ri−r1<0, can be attributed to differences in firm characteristics, particularly the result that dividend-paying firms are larger than no-dividend firms. That being said, we also see that as the fundamental volatility difference increases, our model mechanism's contribution to the risk premium difference increases. For example, whenσi=2σ1, a no-dividend stock's risk premium is 1.82% lower than that of a dividend stock in our model, whereas the corresponding difference is only 0.97% in the benchmark economy. This implies that0.97/1.82=53.5%of our risk premium result can be attributed to the characteristic difference, namely, dividend-paying stocks being large with less volatile fundamentals, with the remaining46.5%due to our mechanism, namely, the no-dividend stock not contributing directly to aggregate consumption.27
However, looking at our return volatility and market beta results, we see that differences in firm characteristics can explain only small fractions of these effects. For instance, even whenσi=2σ1, only 21.7% of our result that the return volatility of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of a dividend stock,σSit−σS1t>0, can be attributed to the characteristic difference, namely, no-dividend stocks having more volatile fundamentals, with the remaining78.3%due to our mechanism, namely, the no-dividend stock price being driven by its estimated pseudo-dividend, which requires estimation. Interestingly, we also see that our result that the market beta of a no-dividend stock is higher than that of a dividend stock,βSit−βS1t>0, cannot be immediately generated in the benchmark economy, which typically generates the wrong sign for this difference, leading to negative values in TableBI. This occurs since in the absence of our mechanism, large stocks (dividend stocks) contribute more to and comove more with the aggregate stock market return, leading their market betas to be higher than those of smaller stocks.
Finally, we also look at the effects of different firm characteristics on our aggregate stock market results. We find that our main results continue to hold and, in fact, become more pronounced under the different firm characteristics that we consider. In particular, whenσi=1.5σ1, we find that the correlation between stock market returns and the aggregate consumption growth rate becomes 0.51 and 0.72 (previously, 0.53 and 0.75) when the no-dividend relative stock size is equal to 21.3% and 14.2%, respectively. We also find that the presence of no-dividend stocks in the market continues to generate a nonmonotonic and particularly negative relation between the stock market risk premium and its volatility for relatively high-volatility periods and a downward-sloping term structure of equity risk premia. For the latter result, the economic magnitude of the effects is significant. For instance, we find that the risk premium of the short-term asset is 41% and 24% higher than the risk premium of the stock market (previously, 18% and 12% higher) when the no-dividend relative stock size is equal to 21.3% and 14.2%, respectively.
